source: Applying Common Context Strategies — Claude Code Skills Training

cards.front: What is progressive disclosure in Claude Code?
cards.back: The process by which Claude Code loads skill information in tiers: YAML front matter first, skill body second, and scripts/references/assets third — only as needed.

cards.front: What is Level 1 in Claude Code's progressive disclosure?
cards.back: The YAML front matter — always loaded for every session. Contains name, description, allowed tools, and other metadata.

cards.front: When does Claude Code load the body of a skill.md file (Level 2)?
cards.back: Only when Claude decides to trigger that skill. The body contains steps, workflows, and instructions.

cards.front: When does Claude Code load scripts, references, and assets (Level 3)?
cards.back: Only when Claude deems them necessary during skill execution. They are optional and loaded on demand.

cards.front: What is the character limit before Claude Code truncates skill descriptions?
cards.back: 250 characters. Front-load the main use case so it appears before the cutoff.

cards.front: What three rules make a skill description effective?
cards.back: 1. Use third person. 2. Include specific trigger phrases. 3. Front-load the main use case in the first 250 characters.

cards.front: What is wrong with this skill description: 'I can help you process Excel files'?
cards.back: It uses first person, is vague, and has no trigger phrases. Better: 'Processes expense CSVs into categorized reports.'

cards.front: Give an example of good trigger phrases for an expense-tracking skill.
cards.back: categorize expenses, expense reports, transaction CSVs, expense summary — concrete, task-specific phrases Claude will match.

cards.front: What is dynamic context injection in Claude Code skills?
cards.back: Using ! commands inside a skill.md file to run bash commands and inject their output as live context at execution time.

cards.front: How would you use dynamic context injection to summarize a pull request?
cards.back: Add !git diff, !gh pr view --comments, and !git diff --name-only in the skill — Claude sees the live output, not the commands.

cards.front: What does the ! prefix do in a Claude Code skill.md file?
cards.back: Runs the following bash command and substitutes its output into the skill context — equivalent to running it in the terminal.

cards.front: Why should you avoid putting heavy content in the YAML front matter?
cards.back: Front matter is loaded for every session regardless of whether the skill is used, so bloating it wastes context budget.
